standard_description: Calculate the expected value of a random variable; interpret it as the mean of the probability distribution.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Task
Bob’s Bagel Shop has estimated the following probabilities for the number of bagels a randomly-selected customer buys when he or she enters the shop.
###TABLE0###
Bob sells bagels for \$0.80 each, or \$9.00 for a dozen. If $X =$ the amount of money Bob collects from a randomly-selected customer, find and interpret the expected value of $X$.

Task
A famous arcade in a seaside resort town consists of many different games of skill and chance.  In order to play a popular “spinning wheel” game at Fred's Fun Factory Arcade, a player is required to pay a small, fixed amount of 25 cents each time he/she wants to make the wheel spin. When the wheel stops, the player is awarded tickets based on where the wheel stops -- and these tickets are then redeemable for prizes at a redemption center within the arcade.
Note: this particular game has no skill component; each spin of the wheel is a random event, and the result from each spin of the wheel is independent of the results of previous spins.
The wheel awards tickets with the following probabilities:
###TABLE0###
(Note: A picture of a wheel fitting these parameters is included.)
If a player were to play this game many, many times, what is the expected (average) number of tickets that the player would win from each spin?
The arcade often provides quarters to its customers in $5.00 rolls. Every day over the summer, a young boy obtains one of these quarter rolls and uses all of the quarters for the spinning wheel game. In the long run, what is the average number of tickets that this boy can expect to win each day using this strategy?
One of the redemption center prizes that the young boy is playing for is a trendy item that costs 300 tickets.  It is also available at a store down the street for $4.99. Without factoring in any enjoyment gained from playing the game or from visiting the arcade, from a strictly monetary point of view, would you advise the boy to try and obtain this item based on arcade ticket winnings or to just go and buy the item at the store?  Explain.
The histogram below summarizes the results of 90 summer days of the boy playing the game using his "$5 roll per day" strategy. The first bar in the histogram represents those days where 40 to 59 tickets were won.
###IMAGE0###
For approximately what percentage of the 90 days did the boy earn fewer than 100 tickets in a day?
For approximately what percentage of the 90 days did the boy earn 200 or more tickets in a day?
For approximately what percentage of the 90 days did the boy earn 300 or more tickets in a day?
To maintain the spinning wheel game machine, the arcade manager adopts a strategy of emptying the money box (that's where the quarters go after they are inserted in the machine) each time she refills the machine with a new roll of tickets. The ticket refill rolls contain 5000 tickets, and the machine is designed to hold $1300 in quarters in its money box. Assume that the machine was fully loaded with 5000 tickets and had an empty money box when it was first used. Using the manager's maintenance strategy, is there any chance that the money box could become completely full with quarters or overflow with quarters? Explain.

The probability distribution for $X =$ the amount of money a player wins (or loses) in a single game is as follows:
###TABLE0###
Compute the expected value of X.
Your friend wants to know if he should play this game many, many times to make some extra money because the 60% chance of winning $5 sounds really good. A common student error is to find the expected number of bagels sold and then
multiply this by 0.8. But because a customer buying 12 bagels receives a discount, this
approach does not lead to a correct answer.
The probability distribution used in this task is artificial and a bit unrealistic. For

a beverage) or who purchases 4 bagels. You might want to have the class develop a
different probability distribution that includes possible values of 0, 1, 2, ..., 12. Students
could then explore several possible assignments of probabilities and assess the impact
on the expected value.
You could also have students explore different pricing strategies and evaluate the
effect on expected money collected per customer.

khan_exercises.title: Transforming random variables
khan_exercises.description: Practice finding the mean and standard deviation of a probability distribution after a linear transformation to a variable.

items.question_content:
Nikola likes to play horseshoes, where players try to score a ringer by throwing a horseshoe so it lands around a post. A player gets $2$ attempts each round. Let $R$ represent the number of ringers that Nikola scores in a round. Here's the probability distribution of $R$ along with summary statistics.

$R=\text{\# of ringers}$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -: | -:
$P(R)$ | $0.75$ | $0.20$ | $0.05$
 |  | Mean: | $\mu_R=0.3$
 |  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_R\approx0.56$

Nikola gets $3$ points for each ringer that he scores. Let $X$ represent the number of points that Nikola gets from ringers in a round.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $X$?**

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] points

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] points
hints:
###Defining $X$ as a transformation of $R$

We can find how many points he gets from ringers by multiplying the number of ringers by $3$:

$X=3R$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $3$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_X&=3\left(\mu_R\right) \\\\
&=3\left(0.3\right) \\\\
&=0.9
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $3$ impacts the standard deviation:

$\begin{align}
\sigma_X&=3\left(\sigma_R\right) \\\\
&\approx3\left(0.56\right) \\\\
&\approx1.68
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A roulette wheel consists of $18$ red slots, $18$ black slots, and $2$ green slots. A player can wager $\$1$ that a marble will land in a red slot, in which case the player would gain a $\$1$. If the marble lands in a slot that isn't red, then the player loses their $\$1$ wager. Let $X$ represent the player's gain from a random $\$1$ wager on red. Here's the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

| Lose | Win |
- | -: | -: 
$X=\text{gain (\$)}$ | $-1$ | $1$
$P(X)$ | $20/38$ | $18/38$ 
 | Mean: | $\mu_X\approx-\$0.05$
 | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx\$1.00$

Suppose a player decides to wager $\$5$ instead of $\$1$, so the possible outcomes become either losing or winning $\$5$. Let $Z$ represent the player's gain from a random $\$5$ wager on red.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $Z$?**

$\mu_Z\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_Z\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $Z$ as a transformation of $X$

We are increasing the wager from $\$1$ to $\$5$, which is equivalent to multiplying both of the possible outcomes by $5$:

$Z=5X$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $5$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Z&=5\left(\mu_X\right) \\\\
&\approx5\left(-0.05\right) \\\\
&\approx-0.25
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $5$ impacts the standard deviation:

$\begin{align}
\sigma_Z&=5\left(\sigma_X\right) \\\\
&\approx5\left(1\right) \\\\
&\approx5
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A carnival has a ring-toss game where players try to toss rings around a stick. A player gets two attempts in a game. Let $X$ represent the number of rings a randomly chosen customer successfully tosses around the stick in a game. Based on previous data, here's the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

$X=\text{\# of successes}$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -: | -:
$P(X)$ | $0.90$ | $0.08$ | $0.02$
 |  | Mean: | $\mu_X=0.12$
 |  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx0.38$

A player wins $10$ tickets for each successful toss. Let $T$ represent the number of tickets a randomly chosen player wins in a game.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $T$?**

$\mu_T=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] tickets

$\sigma_T\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] tickets
hints:
###Defining $T$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find how many tickets they win by multiplying the number of successful tosses by $10$:

$T=10X$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $10$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_T&=10\left(\mu_X\right) \\\\
&=10\left(0.12\right) \\\\
&=1.2
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $10$ impacts the standard deviation:

$\begin{align}
\sigma_T&=10\left(\sigma_X\right) \\\\
&\approx10\left(0.38\right) \\\\
&\approx3.8
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A restaurant offers hamburgers with one, two, or three patties. Let $X$ represent the number of patties a randomly chosen customer orders on their hamburger. Based on previous data, here's the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

$X=\text{\# of patties}$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
- | -: | -: | -:
$P(X)$ | $0.40$ | $0.50$ | $0.10$
 |  | Mean: | $\mu_X=1.7$
 |  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx0.67$

The total price of each burger is set at $\$2$ per patty. Let $T$ represent the total price a randomly chosen customer pays for their burger.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $T$?**

$\mu_T=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_T\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $T$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find the total price of a burger by multiplying the number of patties ordered by $2$:

$T=2X$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $2$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_T&=2\left(\mu_X\right) \\\\
&=2\left(1.7\right) \\\\
&=3.4
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $2$ impacts the standard deviation:

$\begin{align}
\sigma_T&=2\left(\sigma_X\right) \\\\
&\approx2\left(0.67\right) \\\\
&\approx1.34
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A pizza shop sells three sizes of pizza, and they track how often each size gets ordered along with how much they profit from each size. Let $X$ represent the shop's profit on a randomly selected pizza. Here's the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

 | Small | Medium | Large
:- | -: | -: | -: 
$X=\text{profit (\$)}$| $4$ | $8$ | $12$
$P(X)$ | $0.18$ | $0.50$ | $0.32$
|  | Mean: | $\mu_X=\$8.56$
|  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx\$2.77$

The company is going to run a promotion where customers get $\$2$ off any size pizza. Assume that the promotion will not change the probability that corresponds to each size. Let $Y$ represent their profit on a randomly selected pizza with this promotion. 

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $Y$?**

$\mu_Y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_Y\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $Y$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find the profit with this promotion by subtracting $\$2$ from each profit without the promotion:

$Y=X-2$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Subtracting $2$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Y&=\mu_X-2 \\\\
&=8.56-2 \\\\
&=6.56
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Subtracting $2$ does not impact standard deviation — adding or subtracting a constant only shifts data, which doesn't affect the spread.

$\begin{align}
\sigma_Y&=\sigma_X \\\\
&\approx2.77
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A company offers a renters insurance policy that costs a customer $\$70$ per year, and the company will make a payout of $\$10{,}000$ to the customer if they are victim to theft in that year. The company set this price based on the probability of a theft in the area being $0.002$. Let $X$ represent the company's profit from one of these policies. The table below displays the probability distribution and summary statistics for $X$.

 | No theft | Theft |    
- | - | -
$X=\text{profit (\$)}$| $70$ | $-9{,}930$
$P(X)$ | $0.998$ | $0.002$
  | Mean: | $\mu_X=\$50$
  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx\$447$


The company is considering increasing the price of this policy by an additional $\$10$. Let $Y$ represent their profit from one of these higher-priced policies.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $Y$?**

$\mu_Y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_Y\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $Y$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find the profit with this price increase by adding $\$10$ to each possible value of the profit before the price increase:

$Y=X+10$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Adding $10$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Y&=\mu_X+10 \\\\
&=50+10 \\\\
&=60
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Adding $10$ does not impact standard deviation — adding or subtracting a constant only shifts data, which doesn't affect the spread.

$\begin{align}
\sigma_Y&=\sigma_X \\\\
&\approx447
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
A phone manufacturer offers its customers two different versions of the same phone. The low storage version costs $\$350$, and the high storage version costs $\$450$. Let $X$ represent the manufacturer's income on a randomly chosen purchase of this phone. Based on previous data, here's the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

 | Low capacity| High capacity
:- | -: | -: 
$X=\text{income (\$)}$| $350$ | $450$
$P(X)$ | $0.65$ | $0.35$ 
  | Mean: | $\mu_X=\$385$
  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx\$48$

The manufacturer is considering a $\$25$ price increase on both versions of the phone. Assume that this increase will not change the probability that corresponds to each version. Let $Y$ represent their income on a randomly chosen purchase of this higher priced phone.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $Y$?**

$\mu_Y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_Y\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $Y$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find their income from selling a higher priced phone by adding $\$25$ to each of the original prices:

$Y=X+25$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Adding $25$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Y&=\mu_X+25 \\\\
&=385+25 \\\\
&=410
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Adding $25$ does not impact standard deviation — adding or subtracting a constant only shifts data, which doesn't affect the spread.

$\begin{align}
\sigma_Y&=\sigma_X \\\\
&\approx48
\end{align}$

$Y=X-1$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Subtracting $1$ impacts the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Y&=\mu_X-1 \\\\
&=2.88-1 \\\\

items.question_content:
A construction company submitted bids for three contracts. The company estimates that it has a $20\%$ chance of winning any given bid. Let $X$ represent the number of bids the company wins. Here is the probability distribution of $X$ along with summary statistics:

 |  |  |  |  |
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: |
$X=\text{\# of bids won}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$P(X)$ | $0.512$ | $0.384$ | $0.096$ | $0.008$
 |  |  | Mean: | $\mu_X=0.6$
 |  |  | Standard deviation: | $\sigma_X\approx0.69$

It costs the company a total of $\$2{,}000$ to submit these bids, and they stand to gain $\$10{,}000$ for each bid they win. Let $V$ represent their net gain from submitting these three bids.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $V$?**

$\mu_V=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_V\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $V$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find their net gain by subtracting $\$2{,}000$ from $\$10{,}000$ times the number of bids they win:

$V=10{,}000X-2{,}000$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $10{,}000$ and subtracting $2{,}000$ both impact the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_V&=10{,}000\left(\mu_X\right)-2{,}000 \\\\
&=10{,}000\left(0.6\right)-2{,}000 \\\\
&=6{,}000-2{,}000 \\\\
&=4{,}000
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $10{,}000$ impacts the standard deviation, but subtracting $2{,}000$ does not — adding or subtracting a constant only shifts data, which doesn't affect the spread.

$\begin{align}
\sigma_V&=10{,}000\left(\sigma_X\right) \\\\
&\approx10{,}000\left(0.69\right) \\\\
&\approx6{,}900
\end{align}$

 |  |  |  |  |

$P(X)$ | $0.20$ | $0.10$ | $0.40$ | $0.30$
 |  |  | Mean: | $\mu_X=1.8$

It costs Zainab $\$5$ each day to pay for her own transportation to her business, and then each customer she gets pays her $\$60$. Let $Y$ represent Zainab's net gain on a randomly chosen day.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of $Y$?**

$\mu_Y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_Y\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###Defining $Y$ as a transformation of $X$

We can find her income by multiplying the number of customers by $60$, and then subtracting her fixed transportation cost to get her net gain:

$Y=60X-5$

How will this transformation impact the mean and standard deviation?

###Effect on mean

Multiplying by $60$ and subtracting $5$ both impact the mean:

$\begin{align}
\mu_Y&=60\left(\mu_X\right)-5 \\\\
&=60\left(1.8\right)-5 \\\\
&=108-5 \\\\
&=103
\end{align}$

###Effect on standard deviation

Multiplying by $60$ impacts the standard deviation, but subtracting $5$ does not — adding or subtracting a constant only shifts data, which doesn't affect the spread.

$\begin{align}
\sigma_Y&=60\left(\sigma_X\right) \\\\
&\approx60\left(1.08\right) \\\\
&\approx64.8
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
Imani and Samuel are designing a game. In each turn, a player rolls three $6$-sided dice and adds the numbers showing on each face to determine how many points they get that turn. For example, the lowest number of points in a given turn is $1+1+1=3$ points, and the highest is $6+6+6=18$ points.

They let $X$ represent the sum in a given turn, and they find the expected value of $X$ is $E(X)=10.5$ points.

Imani says, "After a very large number of turns, the average number of points per turn will be close to $10.5$."

Samuel says, "Over $100$ turns, we can expect a total of about $1{,}050$ points."

**Whose statement is correct based on the expected value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is expected value?

Expected value is the mean value of a discrete random variable over an infinite number of trials. In other words, the expected value of a discrete random variable is the average value of the variable over many, many trials.

Let's look at each statement to see if it is correct based on the expected value in this context.

###Imani's statement

*"After a very large number of turns, the average number of points per turn will be close to $10.5$."*

Expected value is a long-term average, which is exactly what this statement describes.

This statement is correct based on the expected value.

###Samuel's statement

*"Over $100$ turns, we can expect a total of about $1{,}050$ points."*

Since expected value is a long-term average, we can use it to think about the results of repeated trials. If we think of the expected value as being an average of $10.5$ points per turn, then over $100$ turns, we'd expect $10.5 \cdot 100=1{,}050$ total points, although we may not see this exact total.

This statement is correct based on the expected value.

###Answer

Both statements are correct.

items.question_content:
A certain basketball player regularly shoots sets of $2$ free-throws. The table below displays the probability distribution of $X$, the number of shots the player makes in a set of $2$ attempts.

$X= \# \text{ of shots made}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$
-: | -: | -: | -:
$P(X)$ | $0.36$ | $0.48$ | $0.16$

Priya and Hadley, fans of this player, calculate the expected value of $X$ is $E(X)=0.80$.

Priya says, "The probability that this player makes a free-throw is $0.80$, on average."

Hadley says, "This player will make $0.80$ free-throws in his next set of $2$."

**Whose statement is correct based on the expected value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is expected value?

Expected value is the mean value of a discrete random variable over an infinite number of trials. In other words, the expected value of a discrete random variable is the average value of the variable over many, many trials.

Let's look at each statement to see if it is correct based on the expected value in this context.

###Priya's statement

*"The probability that this player makes a free-throw is $0.80$, on average."*

Expected value is a long-term average, **not** a probability. This player's probability of making a free-throw is actually well below $80\%$.

This statement is **not** correct based on the expected value.

###Hadley's statement

*"This player will make $0.80$ free-throws in his next set of $2$."*

Expected value does **not** tell us about a single set of $2$ free-throws. In any set of $2$ free-throws, the player can make $0$, $1$, or $2$ free-throws — it's not possible for them to make $0.8$ free-throws in a set of $2$ attempts.

This statement is **not** correct based on the expected value.

###Answer

Neither statement is correct.

Expected value is a long-term average, **not** a probability. The probability a given ticket wins a prize is actually well below $89\%$.

This statement is **not** correct based on the expected value.

###Answer

Only Lian's statement is correct.

items.question_content:
A head coach needs to decide whether or not to move forward with a given strategy. Their assistant coach says, "Based on previous data from similar situations, the expected return if we move forward with this strategy is $4.6$ points."

The head coach says, "If we move forward with this strategy, then we'll get $4.6$ points."

The assistant coach says, "If we made this decision in scenarios like this many times, then the average return would be about $4.6$ points."

**Whose statement is correct based on the expected value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is expected value?

Expected value is the mean value of a discrete random variable over an infinite number of trials. In other words, the expected value of a discrete random variable is the average value of the variable over many, many trials.

Let's look at each statement to see if it is correct based on the expected value in this context.

###The head coach's statement

*"If we move forward with this strategy, then we'll get $4.6$ points."*

Expected value does **not** tell us about a single trial. It may not even be possible to get $4.6$ points in this game.

This statement is **not** correct based on the expected value.

###The assistant coach's statement

*"If we made this decision in scenarios like this many times, then the average return would be about $4.6$ points."*

Expected value is a long-term average, which is exactly what this statement describes.

This statement is correct based on the expected value.

###Answer

Only the assistant coach's statement is correct.

items.question_content:
Richard and Cameron buy a carton containing $6$ eggs. The back of the carton says, "The expected number of broken eggs per package is $0.12$."

Richard says, "The probability that one of these cartons contains a broken egg is $0.12$, on average."

Cameron says, "For every $100$ packages like this, we'd predict about $12$ broken eggs in total."

**Whose statement is correct based on the expected value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is expected value?

Expected value is the mean value of a discrete random variable over an infinite number of trials. In other words, the expected value of a discrete random variable is the average value of the variable over many, many trials.

Let's look at each statement to see if it is correct based on the expected value in this context.

###Richard's statement

*"The probability that one of these cartons contains a broken egg is $0.12$, on average."*

Expected value is a long-term average, **not** a probability.

This statement is **not** correct based on the expected value.

###Cameron's statement

*"For every $100$ packages like this, we'd predict about $12$ broken eggs in total."*

Since expected value is a long-term average, we can use it to think about the results of repeated trials. If we think of the expected value as an average of $0.12$ broken eggs per carton, then for every $100$ cartons, we'd expect a total of $0.12 \cdot 100=12$ broken eggs, although we may not see this exact total.

This statement is correct based on the expected value.

###Answer

Only Cameron's statement is correct.

items.question_content:
Michael is a basketball player who regularly shoots sets of $2$ free-throws. Suppose that each shot has probability $0.6$ of being made, and the results of the shots are independent.

The table below displays the probability distribution of $X$, the  number of shots that Michael makes in a set of $2$ attempts.

$X= \# \text{ of makes}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$
- | - | - | -
$P(X)$ | $0.16$ | $0.48$ | $0.36$

**Find the expected value of the number of shots that Michael makes.**

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] makes
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

$X= \# \text{ of makes}$| $\blueD{0}$ | $\greenD1$ | $\purpleD2$
- | - | - | -
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{0.16}$ | $\greenE{0.48}$ | $\purpleE{0.36}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{0}(\blueE{0.16})+\greenD{1}(\greenE{0.48})+\purpleD{2}(\purpleE{0.36}) \\\\
 &= 0+0.48+0.72 \\\\
&=1.2\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(X)=1.2$ makes.

answers.message: Be careful. We don't round the mean (or expected value) of a discrete random variable to a whole number, since it is a long-run average.

items.question_content:
Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on a single slot. If the ball lands in their slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$35$. If the ball doesn't land in their slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a single slot. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$35$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{1}{38}$ | $\dfrac{37}{38}$

**Find the expected value of a player's net gain on a $\$1$ bet on a single slot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$35}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{35}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{35}{38} - \dfrac{37}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(X)= -\dfrac{2}{38}$ dollars or approximately $-0.053$ dollars.

items.question_content:
A data scientist tracked how many cups of coffee she drank every day at work over the course of a year. She used the data to build a probability distribution where the random variable $C$ represents the number of cups of coffee she drank on a given day. Here is the distribution:

 |  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$C=\# \text{ of cups}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$P(C)$ | $0.05$ | $0.10$ | $0.75$ | $0.10$

**Calculate the mean of $C$.**

$\mu_C=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] cups
hints:
##Definition and formula:

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

$C= \# \text{ of cups}$| $\blueD{0}$ | $\greenD1$ | $\purpleD2$ |  $\maroonD{3}$
- | - | - | - | -
$P(C)$ | $\blueE{0.05}$ | $\greenE{0.10}$ | $\purpleE{0.75}$ | $\maroonE{0.10}$

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=}E(C)\\\\
&=\mu_C \\\\
&= \blueD{0}(\blueE{0.15})+\greenD{1}(\greenE{0.10})+\purpleD{2}(\purpleE{0.75})+\maroonD{3}(\maroonE{0.10}) \\\\
 &= 0+0.10+1.50+0.30 \\\\
&=1.9\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_C=1.9$ cups.

items.question_content:
A pizza shop delivers only one kind of pizza, which is sold for $\$10$, and costs the pizza shop $\$6$ to make. The pizza shop has a delivery policy that says if the pizza takes longer than half an hour to arrive, there is no charge. Experience has shown that delivery takes longer than half an hour only $10\%$ of the time.

Let the random variable $X$ be the pizza shop's profit for a randomly selected delivery order for one of these pizzas. Here is the probability distribution for $X$:

 | Late | On-time |
:- | :-: | :-: 
$X=\text{profit}$| $-\$6$ | $\$4$
$P(X)$ | $0.10$ | $0.90$

**Calculate the mean of $X$.**

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula:

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Late | On-time |
:- | :-: | :-: 
$X=\text{gain}$| $\blueD{-\$6}$ | $\greenD{\$4}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{0.10}$ | $\greenE{0.90}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{-6}(\blueE{0.10})+\greenD{4}(\greenE{0.90}) \\\\
 &= -0.6+3.6 \\\\
&=3\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_X=3$ dollars.

items.question_content:
Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on the color red. If the ball lands in a red slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$1$. If the ball doesn't land in a red slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a the color red. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$1$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{18}{38}$ | $\dfrac{20}{38}$

**Calculate the mean of $X$.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$1}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{18}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{20}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{1}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{18}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{20}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{18}{38} - \dfrac{20}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_X= -\dfrac{2}{38}$ or approximately $-0.053$ dollars.

items.question_content:
An electronics retailer offers an optional protection plan for a mobile phone it sells. Customers can choose to buy the protection plan for $\$100$, and in case of an accident, the customer pays a $\$50$ deductible and the retailer will cover the rest of the cost of that repair. The typical cost to the retailer is $\$200$ per repair, and the plan covers a maximum of $3$ repairs.

Let $X$ be the number of repairs a randomly chosen customer uses under the protection plan, and let $F$ be the retailer's profit from one of these protection plans. Based on data from all of its customers, here are the probability distributions of $X$ and $F$:

 |  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of repairs}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$F=\text{ retailer profit}$| $\$100$ | $-\$50$ | $-\$200$ | $-\$350$
**Probability** | $0.90$ | $0.07$ | $0.02$ | $0.01$

**Find the expected value of the retailer's profit per protection plan sold.**

$E(F)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

We're only concerned about the random variable $F$ in this case: 

|  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of repairs}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$F=\text{ retailer profit}$| $\blueD{\$100}$ | $\greenD{-\$50}$ | $\purpleD{-\$200}$ | $\maroonD{-\$350}$
**Probability** | $\blueE{0.90}$ | $\greenE{0.07}$ | $\purpleE{0.02}$ | $\maroonE{0.01}$

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=}E(F)\\\\
&=\mu_F \\\\
&= \blueD{100}(\blueE{0.90})+(\greenD{-50})(\greenE{0.07})+(\purpleD{-200})(\purpleE{0.02})+(\maroonD{-350})(\maroonE{0.01}) \\\\
 &= 90-3.5-4-3.5 \\\\
&=79\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(F)=79$ dollars.

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

We're only concerned about the random variable $X$ in this case:

 |  |  |  | 
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of shots made}$| $\blueD0$ | $\greenD1$ | $\purpleD2$
$M=\text{money gained}$| $-\$5$ | $\$0$ | $\$5$
**Probability** | $\blueE{0.36}$ | $\greenE{0.48}$ | $\purpleE{0.16}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{0}(\blueE{0.36})+\greenD{1}(\greenE{0.48})+\purpleD{2}(\purpleE{0.16}) \\\\
 &= 0+0.48+0.32 \\\\
&=0.8\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_X=0.8$ shots made.

answers.message: Careful, we don't round the mean (or expected value) of a discrete random variable to the nearest whole number, since it is a long-run average.

items.question_content:
Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on a single slot. If the ball lands in their slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$35$. If the ball doesn't land in their slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a single slot. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$35$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{1}{38}$ | $\dfrac{37}{38}$

**Find the expected value of a player's net gain on a $\$1$ bet on a single slot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$35}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{35}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{35}{38} - \dfrac{37}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(X)= -\dfrac{2}{38}$ dollars or approximately $-0.053$ dollars.

items.question_content:
An electronics retailer offers an optional protection plan for a mobile phone it sells. Customers can choose to buy the protection plan for $\$100$, and in case of an accident, the customer pays a $\$50$ deductible and the retailer will cover the rest of the cost of that repair. The typical cost to the retailer is $\$200$ per repair, and the plan covers a maximum of $3$ repairs.

Let $X$ be the number of repairs a randomly chosen customer uses under the protection plan, and let $F$ be the retailer's profit from one of these protection plans. Based on data from all of its customers, here are the probability distributions of $X$ and $F$:

 |  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of repairs}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$F=\text{ retailer profit}$| $\$100$ | $-\$50$ | $-\$200$ | $-\$350$
**Probability** | $0.90$ | $0.07$ | $0.02$ | $0.01$

**Find the expected value of the retailer's profit per protection plan sold.**

$E(F)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

We're only concerned about the random variable $F$ in this case: 

|  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of repairs}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$
$F=\text{ retailer profit}$| $\blueD{\$100}$ | $\greenD{-\$50}$ | $\purpleD{-\$200}$ | $\maroonD{-\$350}$
**Probability** | $\blueE{0.90}$ | $\greenE{0.07}$ | $\purpleE{0.02}$ | $\maroonE{0.01}$

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=}E(F)\\\\
&=\mu_F \\\\
&= \blueD{100}(\blueE{0.90})+(\greenD{-50})(\greenE{0.07})+(\purpleD{-200})(\purpleE{0.02})+(\maroonD{-350})(\maroonE{0.01}) \\\\
 &= 90-3.5-4-3.5 \\\\
&=79\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(F)=79$ dollars.

Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on a single slot. If the ball lands in their slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$35$. If the ball doesn't land in their slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a single slot. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$35$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{1}{38}$ | $\dfrac{37}{38}$

**Find the expected value of a player's net gain on a $\$1$ bet on a single slot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$35}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{35}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{35}{38} - \dfrac{37}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(X)= -\dfrac{2}{38}$ dollars or approximately $-0.053$ dollars.

items.question_content:
Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on the color red. If the ball lands in a red slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$1$. If the ball doesn't land in a red slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a the color red. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$1$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{18}{38}$ | $\dfrac{20}{38}$

**Calculate the mean of $X$.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$1}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{18}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{20}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{1}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{18}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{20}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{18}{38} - \dfrac{20}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_X= -\dfrac{2}{38}$ or approximately $-0.053$ dollars.

items.question_content:
A furniture factory makes a $4$-piece desk. Quality control at the factory has randomly sampled numerous desk sets and determined the number of damaged pieces in each set.

The table below is a  partially completed probability distribution for the random variable $X$, where $X$ represents the number of damaged pieces in a randomly selected desk set from this factory.

 |  |  |  |  | |

$X$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$
$P(X)$ | $0.75$ | $0.14$ | $0.07$ | $0.03$ | $\text{?}$

**What is the probability that a randomly selected desk set contains exactly $4$ damaged pieces?**

$P\left(X=4\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: All of the probabilities in a valid probability distribution must add up to $1$.

Solve for the missing probability:

$\begin{align}0.75+0.14+0.07+0.03+\text{?}&=1 \\
\\
0.99+\text{?}&=1 \\
\\
\text{?}&=0.01\end{align}$

The answer:

$P\left(X=4\right)=0.01$

hints: All of the probabilities in a valid probability distribution must add up to $1$.

Solve for the missing probability:

$\begin{align}
0.475+\text{?}+0.090&=1 \\\\
0.565+\text{?}&=1 \\\\
\text{?}&=0.435
\end{align}$

The answer:

$P\left(Y=1\right)=0.435$

She used the data to build a probability distribution where the random variable $C$ represents the number of cups of coffee she drank on a given day. Here is the partially completed distribution:

 |  |  |  |  |
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$C$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $\geq3$
$P(C)$ | $\text{?}$ | $0.10$ | $0.85$ | $0.05$

**Find the missing probability in the table. **

$P\left(C=0\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: All of the probabilities in a valid probability distribution must add up to $1$.

Solve for the missing probability:

$\begin{align}
\text{?}+0.10+0.85+0.05&=1 \\\\
\text{?}+1&=1 \\\\
\text{?}&=0
\end{align}$

In other words, the given probabilities already add to $1$, so the missing probability must be $0$.

The answer:

$P\left(C=0\right)=0$

topic_path: Mean (expected value) of a discrete random variable

items.question_content:
Michael is a basketball player who regularly shoots sets of $2$ free-throws. Suppose that each shot has probability $0.6$ of being made, and the results of the shots are independent.

The table below displays the probability distribution of $X$, the  number of shots that Michael makes in a set of $2$ attempts.

$X= \# \text{ of makes}$| $0$ | $1$ | $2$
- | - | - | -
$P(X)$ | $0.16$ | $0.48$ | $0.36$

**Find the expected value of the number of shots that Michael makes.**

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] makes
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

$X= \# \text{ of makes}$| $\blueD{0}$ | $\greenD1$ | $\purpleD2$
- | - | - | -
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{0.16}$ | $\greenE{0.48}$ | $\purpleE{0.36}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{0}(\blueE{0.16})+\greenD{1}(\greenE{0.48})+\purpleD{2}(\purpleE{0.36}) \\\\
 &= 0+0.48+0.72 \\\\
&=1.2\end{align}$

##The answer

The expected value is $E(X)=1.2$ makes.

answers.message: Be careful. We don't round the mean (or expected value) of a discrete random variable to a whole number, since it is a long-run average.

items.question_content:
Roulette is a casino game that involves players betting on where a ball will land on a spinning wheel. An American roulette wheel has $38$ numbered slots — half of the slots from $1$ to $36$ are red and the other half are black. Slots $0$ and $00$ are both green.

Suppose that a player bets $\$1$ on a single slot. If the ball lands in their slot, the player gets their initial $\$1$ back plus a payout of $\$35$. If the ball doesn't land in their slot, they lose their $\$1$ bet. Let $X=$ the player's net gain from a $\$1$ bet on a single slot. Here is the probability distribution of $X$:

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\$35$ | $-\$1$
$P(X)$ | $\dfrac{1}{38}$ | $\dfrac{37}{38}$

**Find the expected value of a player's net gain on a $\$1$ bet on a single slot.**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest thousandth.*

$E(X)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]] dollars
hints:
##Definition and formula

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

 | Win | Lose | 
- | - | -
$X=\text{ net gain}$| $\blueD{\$35}$ | $\greenD{-\$1}$
$P(X)$ | $\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}$ | $\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{35}\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{38}}\right)+(\greenD{-1})\left(\greenE{\dfrac{37}{38}}\right) \\\\
 &= \dfrac{35}{38} - \dfrac{37}{38} \\\\
&= -\dfrac{2}{38} \approx -0.053
\end{align}$

##Definition and formula:

In the case of a discrete random variable, **expected value** or mean — denoted as $E(X)$ or $\mu_X$ — is the long-run average outcome. To find expected value, take each value, multiply it by its respective probability, and add up all the products.

$\begin{align}
E(X)=\mu_X &= x_1p_1+x_2p_2+...+x_n p_n \\\\
&= \sum x_ip_i
\end{align}$

##Calculations

We're only concerned about the random variable $X$ in this case:

 |  |  |  | 
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
$X=\# \text{ of shots made}$| $\blueD0$ | $\greenD1$ | $\purpleD2$
$M=\text{money gained}$| $-\$5$ | $\$0$ | $\$5$
**Probability** | $\blueE{0.36}$ | $\greenE{0.48}$ | $\purpleE{0.16}$

$\begin{align} E(X)=\mu_X &= \blueD{0}(\blueE{0.36})+\greenD{1}(\greenE{0.48})+\purpleD{2}(\purpleE{0.16}) \\\\
 &= 0+0.48+0.32 \\\\
&=0.8\end{align}$

##The answer

The mean is $\mu_X=0.8$ shots made.

answers.message: Careful, we don't round the mean (or expected value) of a discrete random variable to the nearest whole number, since it is a long-run average.
